If the work is larger, go ahead and split into worktrees accordingly and coordinate their efforts. Before doing this though please inform the user how you're going to use these subagents.

We want the architecture centered around durable domain code: the layer that defines the core concepts, rules, allowed transitions, invariants, and required side effects independent of any delivery mechanism. Outer layers should stay thin and predictable: translate inputs and outputs, call domain behavior, persist or render results, and surface errors without inventing their own rules.

Keep sources of truth singular, make side effects explicit, and prefer small behavior-owned modules over broad catch-all files. When ownership gets blurry, refactor toward clearer boundaries; when code exists only for speculation, compatibility, or half-owned future surfaces, delete it.

Testing code should be seperate from source code, only combine if you have a good justification to do so.

Please don't use cards for ui, only use them if absolutely necessary.

Once you approach around 1000 LOC in a single file, do an audit of the file and modularize accordingly unless there is a good justification.

<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.
- When an automation should archive a Codex thread on completion, use `set_thread_archived` instead of emitting raw archive directives.

### Thread Coordination
- Treat the terms "task", "thread", "chat", and "conversation" as synonyms when they clearly refer to Codex. Tool names use the term "thread" and Codex uses "task" in the UI. When providing user-facing responses, use "task".
- When the user asks to create, fork, inspect, continue, hand off, pin, archive, rename, or otherwise manage Codex threads, search for the relevant thread tool first: `create_thread`, `fork_thread`, `list_threads`, `read_thread`, `send_message_to_thread`, `handoff_thread`, `set_thread_pinned`, `set_thread_archived`, or `set_thread_title`.
- Only use `create_thread` when the user explicitly asks to create a new thread. Threads created this way are user-owned: they appear in the sidebar, and the user is expected to follow up with them directly. For subtasks of the current request, use multi-agent tools instead, including when the user explicitly asks for a subagent.
- After a successful `create_thread` call, emit `::created-thread{threadId="..."}` for a created thread or `::created-thread{clientThreadId="..."}` for queued worktree setup on its own line in your final response.

### Inline Code Comments
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive when you need to attach feedback directly to specific code lines.
- Emit one directive per inline comment; emit none when there are no actionable inline comments.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3).
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.

<apps_instructions>
## Apps (Connectors)
Apps (Connectors) can be explicitly triggered in user messages in the format `[$app-name](app://{connector_id})`. Apps can also be implicitly triggered as long as the context suggests usage of available apps.
An app is equivalent to a set of MCP tools within the `codex_apps` MCP.
An installed app's MCP tools are either provided to you already, or can be lazy-loaded through the `tool_search` tool. If `tool_search` is available, the apps that are searchable by `tools_search` will be listed by it.
Do not additionally call list_mcp_resources or list_mcp_resource_templates for apps.
</apps_instructions>

<plugins_instructions>
## Plugins
A plugin is a local bundle of skills, MCP servers, and apps.
### How to use plugins
- Skill naming: If a plugin contributes skills, those skill entries are prefixed with `plugin_name:` in the Skills list.
- MCP naming: Plugin-provided MCP tools keep standard MCP identifiers such as `mcp__server__tool`; use tool provenance to tell which plugin they come from.
- Trigger rules: If the user explicitly names a plugin, prefer capabilities associated with that plugin for that turn.
- Relationship to capabilities: Plugins are not invoked directly. Use their underlying skills, MCP tools, and app tools to help solve the task.

<INSTRUCTIONS>
# Global Agent Preferences

These are durable preferences distilled from local agent history. Project instructions and the current user request always take precedence.

## Working Style

- Prefer action once direction is clear. When the user says to go ahead, implement and verify instead of stopping at another proposal.
- For complex, destructive, architectural, or ambiguous work, first explain the root cause or tradeoff, give a concrete plan, and ask targeted questions if the answer changes the approach.
- Do not assume. Inspect the repo, running app, logs, screenshots, or device state directly when the user asks what is happening.
- Keep the user updated with concrete findings, not generic status. Use simple language when explaining failures or architecture.

## Engineering Taste

- Keep implementations as simple as the scope allows. Less is better than more unless there is a strong reason to add machinery.
- Favor long-term maintainable fixes over local patches. Remove dead code, collapse duplicated logic, and simplify tangled flows while fixing bugs.
- When a system feels strung together, consider subtracting or redesigning the flow instead of layering another workaround on top.
- Tests should be useful regression protection, not coverage theater. Prefer the test level that catches real failures quickly, including E2E for important UI, IPC, connection, or workflow behavior.

## Frontend And Product Work

- Check UI work in the running app yourself. Use browser/Playwright/screenshots or available visual tools to verify layout, alignment, interaction, and loading states.
- Strongly prefer polished, minimal, fast interfaces. Avoid boxy/card-heavy layouts when a clean text, list, or section treatment will work.
- Alignment and spacing matter. Fix visible UI details before calling work done, especially responsive wrapping, centered/left-aligned elements, hover states, and clipped content.
- For redesigns, be willing to be bold and creative, but keep the product usable and wired to existing behavior unless the user explicitly allows backend changes.
- Use named frontend/design/performance skills when the user calls them out, and apply them before implementing.

## Git And Delivery

- Commit, push, merge, or create branches only when the user asks. When asked to commit, verify first and report anything left uncommitted.
- Use a new branch for risky, destructive, or experimental work when requested. Keep focused fixes on the smallest reasonable change surface.
- If CI matters for the request, keep going until it is green or explain the blocker with the exact failing check.
- For firmware, hardware, or device workflows, upload/run/monitor directly when possible and report what happened, including verification gaps.

## Output Expectations

- Summaries should lead with what changed, what was verified, and what remains risky.
- For bug investigations, include the root cause and why the previous behavior failed before describing the fix.
- For plans, make them concrete enough to execute: files or areas to inspect, implementation steps, validation steps, and rollback or migration notes when relevant.

--- project-doc ---

This product is still in pre-alpha and we have zero users and zero valuable data. Every change should be destructive, no legacy or backwards compatible bullshit, this application has no users or releases, treat it as such. If you add backwards compatibility, I will go apeshit on your dumbass.

Maintainer = owner or members

When coding, feel free to spin up medium-level subagents to get non-overlapping code work done and then review outputs at the end. If the work is larger, go ahead and split into worktrees accordingly and coordinate their efforts. Before doing this though please inform the user how you're going to use these subagents.

We want the architecture centered around durable domain code: the layer that defines the core concepts, rules, allowed transitions, invariants, and required side effects independent of any delivery mechanism. Outer layers should stay thin and predictable: translate inputs and outputs, call domain behavior, persist or render results, and surface errors without inventing their own rules.

Keep sources of truth singular, make side effects explicit, and prefer small behavior-owned modules over broad catch-all files. When ownership gets blurry, refactor toward clearer boundaries; when code exists only for speculation, compatibility, or half-owned future surfaces, delete it.

Testing code should be seperate from source code, only combine if you have a good justification to do so.

Please don't use cards for ui, only use them if absolutely necessary.

Once you approach around 1000 LOC in a single file, do an audit of the file and modularize accordingly unless there is a good justification. 

In general, I trust you with refactors as they don't effect the behavior of the application. However, on behavior making changes I want to be very involved and make sure we go slow and methodically. 

Autoreview timeout should be set to 15 minutes

</INSTRUCTIONS>

payload.message: I’m continuing the approved `ab-implement` pass on `codex/maintainability-reduction`. I’m also putting the three available reaper agents on non-overlapping closeout checks—architecture gaps, tooling duplication, and behavior/test coverage—while I finish the remaining reductions and integration verification.

The implementation is structurally sound so far, and the benchmark harness has dropped from 945 to 297 lines while retaining authenticated reads and a real first-push workflow. I found two closeout items before verification: the new harness needs a temp-directory fix, and four production paths still rebuild the entire catalog; I’m replacing those with focused queries so the Postgres-only architecture is real rather than cosmetic. The extra reaper slots were unavailable, so I’m handling those checks directly.

Scope VCS data architecture
Changes Current state Pressure points North star Roadmap Phase 6 start

Planning artifact

Data architecture lay of the land

Prepared 2026-06-30 Revised Phase 5 start Phase 6 start
Linear project: Data Architecture Improvements Repo README Post-pre-alpha notes

Executive read

Scope has a strong domain center, but the persistence and read side still behave like a serialized catalog with large repository aggregates. The next architecture push should keep domain behavior authoritative while breaking storage, reads, and side effects into smaller durable facts. Scope now has a stronger domain core, typed facts, read models, and a real worker service. The final architecture push should keep those boundaries intact while making Git and object-store IO bounded, observable, and willing to push back under load.

Current recommendation: Phase 4 is merged on main. Move next to ADA-38: introduce a transactional outbox and a dedicated scope-worker Railway service, starting with worker-owned read-model rebuild jobs. Phase 5 is merged on main. Move next to ADA-39: bound Git subprocess and object-store IO, starting with explicit service-level budgets and predictable backpressure on Git hot paths.

Phase 6 target: request handlers should stop owning durable side-effect execution. Commit explicit work with canonical facts, then let a worker service claim, execute, retry, and report jobs without making derived data canonical. request handlers should stop admitting unbounded Git, object-store, projection-build, and blob-buffer work. Bound concurrency, size, timeout, and failure behavior first; stream only after the bounded shape is explicit.

Phase 6 transition

This revision moves the plan from read-model materialization into worker-owned side effects. Phase 4 proved live file-tree read models can be rebuilt from canonical facts; Phase 5 should now move rebuild execution behind a transactional outbox and a deployable worker service. This revision moves the plan from worker-owned read-model side effects into bounded IO. Phase 5 proved the outbox, scope-core, and scope-worker shape; Phase 6 should now make remaining expensive request-path IO explicit, budgeted, observable, and willing to return backpressure instead of stalling.

Added or newly accepted plan text Deleted or replaced plan text

Defaults accepted

  • Phase 0 local benchmark work is merged on main and available through ./dev/scope-dev bench.
  • Phase 1 is now the active work: ADA-34 removes database writes from normal authenticated request paths. Phase 1 is complete on main: normal Clerk/CLI read authorization no longer performs snapshot refreshes or session last-used writes.
  • Phase 2 is now the active work: ADA-35 replaces the global metadata lock with scoped transactional invariants. Phase 2 is merged: cleanup queues now persist as typed SQL rows with idempotent upserts, generation fences, attempts, next-run timing, completion state, and diagnostics.
  • Phase 3 is now the active work: ADA-36 normalizes repository persistence around domain-owned tables while preserving destructive pre-alpha reset semantics. Phase 3 is merged: repository settings, first-push tokens, git-push tokens, git-clone tokens, and live git snapshots now persist as typed fact rows.
  • Phase 4 is now the active work: ADA-37 materializes read models, starting with live projection file trees keyed by repo revision and audience class. Phase 4 is merged: live projection file-tree read models now persist by repo revision, source, audience class, and bounded path key, with route fallback when rows are stale or missing.
  • Phase 5 is now the active planning topic: ADA-38 introduces transactional outbox jobs and a dedicated Railway worker service, starting with read-model rebuild execution. Phase 5 is merged: the root Rust workspace now has shared crates/scope-core, thin api and worker services, transactional outbox jobs, worker-owned read-model rebuilds, local worker startup, and scope-worker Railway CI deployment.
  • Phase 6 is now the active planning topic: ADA-39 bounds Git subprocess and object-store IO before broad streaming rewrites.
  • The local benchmark harness now mints a fresh local-dev CLI session for authenticated and push cases, so stale workstation tokens no longer hide benchmark coverage.
  • Roles should be settled enough for table vocabulary now: public, member/maintainer capabilities, and owner.
  • Read models should start as audience-class based, not user-specific.
  • History should remain product/projected history for now, not full audit history.
  • Bound Git and object-store work before doing broad streaming rewrites.

Now deferred

Do not move every side effect at once inside ADA-38. Start with the outbox contract, worker service shell, and one safe job family: projection read-model rebuilds. Cleanup, import, publish, and Git artifact jobs follow after the worker loop is proven. Do not jump straight to a broad async streaming rewrite in ADA-39. Start with resource budgets, admission control, subprocess timeouts, bounded object-store buffers, and clear 429/503-style backpressure. Streaming APIs can follow only where the bounded interface still needs them.

Phase 0 result

The baseline does not yet expose internal lock wait or SQL query counts, but it gives us a repeatable local measurement loop. It now serves as a regression guard while ADA-38 moves work out of request handlers; it is not expected to prove a speedup until larger fixtures and more worker-owned job families exist. It now serves as a regression guard while ADA-39 adds budgets and backpressure; speedup is not the primary expectation, but burst behavior should become more predictable under saturation. Internal counters stay on the instrumentation backlog.

Merged

Local harness

bench/phase0.mjs runs through ./dev/scope-dev bench and writes ignored JSON/Markdown reports under .tmp/bench/phase0/.

Auth read

List repos

Latest local run: auth list repos p50 2.33ms 2.01ms, p95 2.68ms 2.47ms, burst p95 4.19ms 4.05ms. Phase 5 should keep this as a regression guard while moving derived work out of request handling. Phase 6 should keep this as a regression guard while adding admission limits to heavier Git and object-store paths.

Git write

Receive-pack

First push with disposable generated repos measured p50 75.24ms 75.50ms, p95 146.77ms 151.14ms. This remains useful as a regression guard, not the first Phase 5 target. This remains useful as both a regression guard and a Phase 6 saturation target.

Gap

Lock visibility

The harness reports external latency and status mix today. Phase 5 should use schema tests, enqueue/claim/retry tests, worker route-fallback tests, and CI deployment checks as primary proof; the harness is a regression signal, not the success metric. Phase 6 should use saturation tests, timeout tests, body-size tests, subprocess-failure tests, and burst benchmark status mix as primary proof; median latency alone is not the success metric.

Phase 1 result

ADA-34 is now implemented on main. The important architectural change is not external latency alone; it is that established authenticated reads no longer become hidden metadata writes.

Merged

Read-first Clerk auth

Known Clerk identities resolve through a read-only lookup and return a request-scoped identity snapshot. Missing identities still bootstrap through the explicit write path.

Merged

Read-only CLI verification

Valid CLI session use no longer refreshes last_used_at_unix or deletes expired sessions during normal request authorization.

Guarded

Lifecycle writes remain explicit

CLI login, exchange grant creation, device login completion, and revocation still use reconciled Clerk snapshots where later CLI auth depends on stored user state.

Next

Global lock is exposed

With auth reads removed from the write-lock path, ADA-35 can focus on the remaining broad lock users: repo mutations, cleanup queues, metadata reset, and lifecycle writes.

Phase 2 result

ADA-35 landed as the cleanup-row slice of the lock-decomposition work. It did not remove every metadata lock caller, but it replaced the most awkward lock-row queue state with typed, inspectable, generation-fenced SQL rows.

Merged

Typed cleanup rows

scope_repo_storage_cleanup_jobs and scope_source_blob_cleanup_jobs now store pending cleanup state outside scope_metadata_locks.

Guarded

Idempotent retries

Cleanup queue writes use upserts, attempts, next-run timing, last error, completion state, and generation fences so stale drain attempts cannot complete newer cleanup work.

Benchable

Fresh local auth

./dev/scope-dev bench now bootstraps a local-dev CLI session for the seeded user, so authenticated routes and push benchmarks run without relying on stale workstation CLI state.

Carry forward

Lock remains

Repository mutations, repo lifecycle writes, admin/reset paths, and explicit auth lifecycle flows still use the metadata lock. Phase 5 should not pretend workers solve all locking; it only moves explicit side-effect execution out of request handlers. Repository mutations, repo lifecycle writes, admin/reset paths, and explicit auth lifecycle flows still use the metadata lock. Phase 6 should not pretend IO budgets solve all locking; they only prevent expensive IO from consuming unbounded request capacity.

Current state

Recent work has cleaned up several boundaries: object-store modules are split, staged push rules moved into domain ownership, and collaboration HTTP glue was condensed. The remaining data problem is less about file size and more about the shape of state and side effects.

Strong

Domain model

StoredRepository, SourceGraph, visibility policy, staged updates, collaboration rules, and projections hold the important product rules in durable domain code.

Mixed

Persistence

Postgres has real tables for users, auth identities, members, invites, CLI auth, repositories, typed cleanup jobs, repository fact rows, live projection read models, and outbox jobs. Repository and auth lifecycle mutations still coordinate through the metadata lock, and worker execution is not yet separated. Repository and auth lifecycle mutations still coordinate through the metadata lock, but read-model rebuild execution is now separated through scope-worker. The repository table still stores graph, policy, tokens, pending import, snapshot, and staged update as JSON. The repository table still stores graph, policy, visibility events, pending import, and staged update as JSON; settings, repo tokens, clone tokens, and live git snapshots are typed fact rows.

Costly

Reads

Repo detail, files, projection preview, history, and Git clone paths commonly rehydrate the full repository aggregate and replay projection logic on demand. The file-list route can now use a live projection read model, but repo detail, projection preview, history, and Git clone paths still commonly rehydrate the full repository aggregate and replay projection logic on demand.

Hot

Side effects

Git subprocesses, object-store reads and writes, projection Git repo creation, bundle restore, import/publish work, and cleanup can still run inline with request handling. Cleanup and read-model state are now represented by typed rows, but execution is still API-owned rather than worker-owned. Read-model rebuilds are worker-owned now; Phase 6 targets the remaining unbounded request-path IO before moving more job families to workers.

domain/store.rs domain/projection.rs domain/projection_views.rs db/schema.rs db/mod.rs db/locks.rs db/cleanup_queue.rs db/outbox.rs git/upload.rs git/storage.rs object_store/mod.rs

Typical hot path today

This is the pattern I would expect to dominate current latency and capacity. It is not wrong for pre-alpha, but it is the shape that will cap growth.

Auth
Clerk JWT is verified, then resolve_clerk_user can enter a metadata write transaction to create or update the Scope user snapshot. Established Clerk and CLI principals now resolve through read-oriented paths. Explicit lifecycle flows still reconcile user snapshots where stored auth state depends on them.
Metadata
Repo reads take the metadata lock row in share mode. Mutations and repo lifecycle, cleanup, reset, and explicit auth lifecycle flows take it in update mode. repo lifecycle, repo aggregate mutations, reset, cleanup drain reconciliation, and explicit auth lifecycle flows still take it in update mode. Many unrelated repos therefore still coordinate through one row.
Aggregate
A repository row is loaded and decoded into StoredRepository, including graph, policy, visibility events, pending import, and staged updates. This aggregate decode is the main Phase 3 target. Phase 3 moved settings, token state, clone tokens, and live snapshot pointers out of this aggregate; Phase 5 moved read-model rebuild execution to workers; Phase 6 targets unbounded IO rather than another broad aggregate split.
Compute
Projection, file tree, preview, and history are computed from the source graph on request. Public and owner views often repeat the same work.
Git and blobs
Git clone paths may build or restore bare repos using subprocesses and object-store reads. Object-store calls currently return whole byte buffers. This is now the Phase 6 focus: bound admission, subprocess runtime, object sizes, and parallel object-store work before changing the storage interface shape.

Pressure points

These are the places where the architecture is likely to bend first as data or concurrency grows.

Area Current behavior Why it matters Likely fix
Global metadata lock One scope_metadata_locks row protects broad metadata invariants. Normal auth reads are no longer the issue, but unrelated repos can still queue behind repo mutations, cleanup updates, metadata reset, and explicit lifecycle writes. Normal auth reads and cleanup queue storage are no longer the core issue, but unrelated repos can still queue behind repo mutations, cleanup drain reconciliation, metadata reset, and explicit lifecycle writes. Use constraints, row-level locks, scoped repo locks, and typed job tables instead of one catalog lock. Phase 5 should avoid holding request-owned transaction scope while executing rebuild work, but scoped locking remains a later storage/invariant pass. Phase 6 should avoid holding scarce IO budget while waiting on metadata work, but scoped locking remains a separate storage/invariant pass.
Large repository JSON scope_repositories stores graph, policy, visibility events, tokens, snapshots, pending import, and staged update as JSON. scope_repositories still stores graph, policy, visibility events, pending import, and staged update as JSON; settings, tokens, clone tokens, and snapshot pointers are typed rows. Normal detail/list/settings reads become sensitive to repo history size and JSON decode cost. Split canonical facts into domain-owned tables and rehydrate full aggregate only for mutation paths. This is partly landed; defer the next canonical split unless Phase 5 worker boundaries require a smaller fact surface. This is partly landed; defer the next canonical split unless Phase 6 budget work exposes a concrete oversized aggregate problem.
Derived reads Live file trees have a disposable read model, but projection preview, history, file diffs, and clone artifacts are still built from canonical graph data on demand. Live file trees have a disposable worker-rebuilt read model, but projection preview, history, file diffs, and clone artifacts are still built from canonical graph data on demand. Read cost tracks total repo history instead of result size. Materialize read models keyed by repo revision, source, and audience class.
Inline Git work Git subprocesses and bundle/projection repo creation happen during request handling. A burst of pushes or clones can block normal UI traffic. Add outbox jobs, workers, prepared artifacts, concurrency limits, and explicit backpressure responses. Add named concurrency budgets, subprocess timeouts, bounded request bodies, stderr/output truncation, and explicit backpressure responses. Move more Git artifacts to workers only after admission control is boring.
Object-store interface ObjectStore::get returns Vec<u8>; S3 uses blocking reqwest inside block_in_place. Large blobs and parallel Git operations can put memory and blocking thread capacity under pressure. Introduce bounded read/write APIs, streaming where useful, and one service-level IO budget. Introduce one service-level IO budget first, enforce maximum object sizes, and isolate blocking S3 calls. Streaming should be a second cut for paths that still need large-object throughput.
Cleanup queues Pending source blob and repo storage deletions live as JSON on the metadata lock row. Pending source blob and repo storage deletions now live in typed cleanup job rows. Cleanup state shares contention with unrelated metadata work and is hard to retry or inspect. Cleanup state is inspectable, but cleanup execution and reconciliation are still API-owned and can still participate in broad metadata transactions. Keep the typed rows; later phases should let workers execute them through an explicit queue contract. Keep the typed rows. Phase 6 may wrap cleanup object-store deletes in the shared IO budget, but moving cleanup execution to workers is not the first ADA-39 slice.

North star

The target architecture should preserve the thing Scope already does well: domain behavior owns the rules. The change is that persistence stores durable facts and read models serve repeated queries.

Domain behavior Owns invariants, transitions, access decisions, projection semantics, and required effects.
Canonical facts Repo record, settings, commits, file changes, visibility events, blobs, tokens, members, invites, staged updates.
Read models Current file trees, history summaries, file diffs, preview metrics, access summaries, prepared Git refs.
Workers Import, publish, read-model rebuild, Git artifact generation, source blob cleanup, repo storage cleanup. Run these from a dedicated Railway worker service rather than the request-serving API. The dedicated scope-worker service now owns read-model rebuilds; other job families should migrate after Phase 6 makes resource admission explicit.
Thin delivery HTTP, CLI, Git smart HTTP, and web routes translate inputs, call domain operations, persist facts, and render results.

Canonical write model

  • Keep source graph and policy canonical, but store them as rows instead of one large JSON document.
  • Require every mutation to produce a repo revision and explicit effects.
  • Commit facts and outbox jobs in the same transaction.
  • Rehydrate a full aggregate only where domain behavior needs the whole aggregate to make a transition.

Disposable read model

  • Treat projection trees, history pages, file diffs, and Git artifacts as rebuildable products of canonical facts.
  • Key read models by repo id, repo revision, source (live or review), and audience class.
  • Return explicit rebuilding or unavailable states when the read model is stale or missing.
  • Version read-model semantics so old caches can be deleted destructively.

Table direction

This is not a final schema. Phase 2 used this vocabulary for cleanup rows, Phase 3 used it for repository fact rows, and Phase 4 used it for disposable projection read models. Phase 5 should now apply the same ownership vocabulary to transactional outbox jobs and worker execution. Phase 2 used this vocabulary for cleanup rows, Phase 3 used it for repository fact rows, Phase 4 used it for disposable projection read models, and Phase 5 used it for transactional outbox jobs and worker execution. Phase 6 should not add many tables; it should make runtime resource ownership explicit in code and tests. The important part is ownership: each table should either be canonical domain fact, disposable read model, or explicit side-effect work.

Group Candidate tables Purpose
Repository identity repo_records, repo_settings, repo_revisions Small rows for list/detail/settings routes. Revision becomes the join point between canonical changes, read models, and job idempotency. Start Phase 3 here if we need a low-risk first cut.
Access repo_members, repo_invites, repo_access_snapshots Members and invites are already partly normalized. Access snapshots can keep UI and Git authorization from needing the full source graph.
Auth and tokens auth_identities, cli_sessions, repo_push_tokens, repo_clone_tokens Move repo token state out of repository JSON. Normal auth verification should be a narrow indexed lookup, not a catalog write. Repo tokens are a good Phase 3 candidate because their current JSON shape is independent from most source graph semantics.
Source graph logical_commits, logical_commit_parents, file_changes, source_blobs Store the canonical source graph in queryable rows. Preserve domain ownership by writing through domain transition outputs.
Visibility visibility_rules, visibility_events, path_visibility_epochs Make current visibility and historical visibility inspectable. Epochs can support fast public history and audit semantics.
Review state pending_imports, pending_import_files, staged_updates, staged_file_changes Keep review surfaces explicit instead of stuffing pending work into the repo row.
Read models projection_files, projection_commits, history_entries, file_diff_index Serve UI and API reads in O(page size or result size), not O(total repo history). Live projection file trees are landed; Phase 5 should make rebuild execution worker-owned before broadening to projected commits, history pages, or diff indexes. Live projection file trees and worker-owned rebuilds are landed; Phase 6 should not broaden read-model coverage unless IO budget work exposes a small, necessary read-model boundary.
Git artifacts git_snapshots, git_projection_artifacts, git_artifact_refs Track prepared bundles, bare repo cache keys, snapshot blobs, and artifact readiness.
Work queues outbox_jobs, cleanup_jobs, job_attempts plus the landed scope_repo_storage_cleanup_jobs and scope_source_blob_cleanup_jobs. Make side effects explicit, idempotent, retryable, and visible to operators. Phase 5 should add a transactional outbox envelope for new worker-owned jobs while preserving typed cleanup rows until their execution path is migrated deliberately. The outbox envelope is landed for projection read-model rebuilds. Phase 6 should leave new job families alone unless a budgeted Git or object-store path has to enqueue explicit work to stay predictable.
Worker service deployment api crate shared modules, worker binary, service-specific Railpack config, and GitHub Actions deploy workflow. Root Rust workspace, shared crates/scope-core library, thin api service crate, thin worker service crate, service-specific Railpack configs, and GitHub Actions deploy workflows. Phase 5 should make scope-worker deploy like scope-api, scope-cli, and scope-web: CI first, Railway deploy second, no manual service drift. The structure should prevent the API crate from becoming the worker's implicit shared library. scope-worker now deploys as its own service. Phase 6 should keep budget/runtime code shared intentionally, not by making the worker depend on HTTP delivery code.
Resource budgets RuntimeBudgets, named Git semaphores, named object-store semaphores, body/object size limits, subprocess timeouts, and backpressure errors. Make expensive IO admission a first-class service boundary. This is code/config/test ownership, not a new canonical data model.

Roadmap

I would treat the Linear project as a sequence of small destructive changes. Each step should leave the app working, but we should not preserve old storage paths for compatibility.

Phase 0

ADA-33 - Benchmark hot paths

Build a repeatable simulated benchmark for auth, repo list, repo detail, files, projection preview, history, clone credential, info/refs, upload-pack, receive-pack, publish, and cleanup. Complete: local benchmark harness is merged and available through ./dev/scope-dev bench.

Baseline is available for external latency, response bytes, status mix, Git smart HTTP, git subprocess, and first-push receive-pack timing.
Phase 1

ADA-34 - Remove auth writes from normal reads

Active now: resolve established Clerk users and valid CLI sessions through read-only lookups. Move user snapshot refresh, expired-session cleanup, and last-used writes out of the normal request path. Complete: established Clerk and CLI auth reads no longer perform normal request-path snapshot refreshes, expired-session cleanup, or last-used writes. Explicit lifecycle writes remain named and tested.

Success means normal authenticated GETs do not take the metadata write lock just to know who the principal is. This was the smallest cut that made the global-lock problem easier to see.
Phase 2

ADA-35 - Replace the global metadata lock

Active now: audit every metadata lock caller, move uniqueness and referential integrity into constraints where possible, lock individual repo rows for repo mutations, and move cleanup state toward typed rows. Complete for this slice: cleanup queue state moved out of metadata-lock JSON and into typed SQL rows. The broader lock-decomposition idea remains valid, but remaining lock callers should be narrowed opportunistically as repository facts are normalized.

Success means unrelated repos can read and mutate without queuing behind a catalog-wide lock row. Do not split repository JSON in this phase unless it is necessary to remove the lock safely. Success means cleanup work is typed, retryable, inspectable, and benchmarked without stale local auth. It does not mean all metadata lock callers are gone.
Phase 3

ADA-36 - Normalize repository persistence

Active now: split the large repository JSON aggregate into durable fact tables. Keep mutation APIs behavior-owned: SQL stores facts, but domain modules still decide transitions and side effects. Complete for this slice: settings, repo token state, clone tokens, and live git snapshot pointers moved into typed fact tables while domain transitions stayed authoritative.

Use destructive pre-alpha reset. Delete obsolete JSON columns instead of shadow-writing old and new shapes. Start with a narrow slice that proves the encode/decode boundary before splitting the full source graph. The remaining repository JSON fields are intentionally left for later canonical fact splits; Phase 4 should not continue that work unless a read-model boundary needs it.
Phase 4

ADA-37 - Materialize read models

Active now: precompute live projection file trees by repo revision and audience class. Keep projected commits, history summaries, and file diff indexes as the next read-model expansions. Complete for this slice: live file-tree read models are materialized by repo revision and audience class, with stale/missing fallback to domain projection.

Normal file-list reads should become indexed reads first. Rebuild code should delete and recreate rows from canonical repo facts, not mutate read models directly. Phase 5 moved rebuild work out of request-owned repository saves because the route already had a safe fallback path.
Phase 5

ADA-38 - Add outbox and workers

Active now: commit outbox jobs with metadata changes and run a separate scope-worker Railway service. Start with projection read-model rebuild jobs before import, publish, cleanup, or Git artifact execution. Complete: canonical repository saves now enqueue projection read-model rebuild jobs, scope-worker claims and retries them, and the root workspace keeps shared domain/runtime code in crates/scope-core.

This phase includes a narrow shared-runtime refactor, a durable claim/retry job contract, local dev worker startup, and CI/Railway deployment for scope-worker. The worker is real, but only one job family moved. That is enough infrastructure for Phase 6 to focus on IO admission instead of more service restructuring.
Phase 6

ADA-39 - Bound Git and object-store IO

Add service-level concurrency budgets for receive-pack, upload-pack, projection builds, raw snapshot restore, bundle generation, and object-store operations. Active now: add service-level resource budgets for receive-pack, upload-pack, projection builds, raw snapshot restore, bundle generation, and object-store operations. Return explicit backpressure before requests consume unbounded memory, subprocess, or blocking-thread capacity.

The user-visible behavior should be predictable backpressure rather than random request stalls. This is the final planned data-architecture phase: bound first, stream only where the bounded API proves insufficient.

Phase 6 work order

The first Phase 5 PR should be a narrow worker/outbox slice, not a rewrite of import, publish, cleanup, and Git artifact execution all at once. Preserve domain behavior, commit jobs transactionally, and prove one disposable job family can move out of request handling. The Phase 6 PR should be a narrow bounded-IO slice, not a rewrite of Git storage, S3 streaming, import processing, or artifact workers. Preserve existing behavior where admitted; make overload deterministic where not admitted.

1

Inventory expensive IO

Start with projection read-model rebuilds because they are disposable, idempotent by repo version, and already protected by route fallback when rows are stale or missing. Map every Git and object-store hot path: receive-pack, upload-pack, projection bare repo builds, snapshot restore, bundle generation, import cat-file work, source blob reads/writes, and cleanup deletes.

2

Define runtime budgets

Create an explicit job envelope with kind, idempotency key, target repo/version, payload, state, attempt count, next run time, lease fields, timestamps, and last error. Add a single service-owned budget object with named semaphores, size limits, and timeouts. Configure defaults through env, wire it through API state, and keep domain rules independent from admission mechanics.

3

Bound Git execution

Add a worker binary/service that claims jobs, runs handlers, records success or retryable failure, and deploys through its own Railway service and GitHub Actions workflow. Centralize Git command execution behind budget acquisition, subprocess timeout, max request/body handling, output/error truncation, and deterministic cleanup of staging directories.

Implementation rule: no Phase 5 worker job is allowed to invent product state. API/domain writes own canonical facts and enqueue jobs in the same transaction; workers only execute explicit, idempotent side effects from those facts. no Phase 6 budget is allowed to change domain semantics. An admitted operation should behave as before; a rejected operation should fail early with a clear retryable/backpressure error.

Guardrails

These are the architectural rules I would hold while doing the data work.

Keep

  • Domain modules own rules, allowed transitions, visibility semantics, and required side effects.
  • Effects are explicit values before they become jobs, object deletes, notifications, or artifact builds.
  • Read models are rebuildable from canonical facts and versioned by semantics.
  • Expensive IO has named budgets, clear timeouts, and predictable retryable failure states.
  • Tests target behavior and failure modes: permissions, visibility leaks, stale reads, idempotent retries, and cleanup safety.

Reject

  • Backward compatibility layers for obsolete pre-alpha storage shapes.
  • SQL branches that independently invent business rules already owned by domain code.
  • Read-model tables that become a second canonical source of truth.
  • Inline long-running Git, S3, or cleanup work when a transaction can enqueue explicit work instead. Unbounded Git subprocess, S3, object-buffer, or cleanup work inside request handling.

Resolved decisions

These choices are no longer open for the current plan. They should shape the first implementation passes unless we deliberately reopen them later.

Decision Answer Plan effect Status
Role model before data split Use the default: settle enough vocabulary now for public, member/maintainer capabilities, and owner. Access tables and read-model audience classes should be named around durable concepts, not temporary UI labels. Accepted
Projection audience classes Use the default: start with owner/private-reader and public; avoid per-user read models unless member-specific visibility appears. Per-user read models explode cardinality. Current members all read private files once published, so audience classes are enough. Accepted
History semantics Use the default: keep history as product/projected history for now, not full audit history. Audit semantics require different storage and permissions than user-facing public history. Accepted
Worker runtime Start inside the API process behind a feature/config boundary. Run workers as their own Railway service, with a codebase refactor and CI deploy path similar to the other services. The project now includes shared-library extraction, scope-worker binary ownership, Railway service configuration, and GitHub Actions deployment. Implemented
Streaming sequence Use the default: bound first, stream second. Add concurrency budgets and size limits before broad async object-store rewrites. Streaming is useful, but an unbounded streaming system still overloads. Budgeting gives immediate safety. Active

Conversation agenda

The Phase 5 conversation should focus on worker ownership: which side effect moves first, what the transactional job envelope guarantees, how workers claim/retry jobs, and how Railway deploys the worker without manual drift. The Phase 6 conversation should focus on resource ownership: which IO paths can consume request capacity, what budgets exist, how overload fails, and where streaming is genuinely needed after limits are explicit.

  1. Inventory side-effect surfaces: read-model rebuild, cleanup queues, import processing, publish/apply work, Git projection artifacts, and object-store deletes. Inventory IO surfaces: receive-pack, upload-pack, projection bare repos, snapshot restore, bundle generation, import parsing, source blob reads/writes, and cleanup deletes.
  2. Pick the first worker job: projection read-model rebuild, keyed by repo id, repo version, source, and audience class semantics. Pick first budgets: receive-pack, upload-pack, Git projection/build work, and object-store operations.
  3. Keep source graph, policy, and repo facts canonical; outbox rows are commands to derive or clean up state, not product state themselves. Keep source graph, policy, and repo facts canonical; budgets decide admission and failure shape, not product rules.
  4. Use schema inventory, transactional enqueue tests, worker claim/retry tests, route fallback tests, local clippy/tests, CI workflow checks, and the Phase 0 harness as primary proof for ADA-38. Use timeout tests, body-size tests, saturation tests, subprocess-failure tests, local clippy/tests, and the Phase 0 harness as primary proof for ADA-39.
  5. The next cut is chosen: enqueue read-model rebuild jobs transactionally and let scope-worker execute them. The next cut is chosen: centralize budget acquisition and apply it first to Git smart HTTP and object-store hot paths.
  6. Keep cleanup, import, publish, and Git artifact execution deferred until the first worker loop is landed and observable. Keep broad streaming, artifact-worker migration, and full import/publish rewrites deferred unless a bounded path cannot be made predictable without them.

How I will start Phase 6

Phase 5 should move side-effect execution out of request handlers without weakening domain-owned transitions. The work starts with a transactional outbox and worker-owned read-model rebuilds, not import/publish rewrites or cleanup execution. Phase 6 should make expensive IO predictable without weakening Git, projection, or object-store behavior. The work starts with admission budgets and bounded execution, not a wholesale streaming rewrite.

Start 1

Inventory IO ownership

Introduce a root Rust workspace with a shared crates/scope-core library and thin service crates for api and worker. Move durable domain, persistence, database, object-store, Git, and job contracts into scope-core; keep HTTP routes, response types, TypeScript export glue, and request-only auth adapters in api; keep polling, job claiming, job handlers, and worker lifecycle in worker. Audit Git subprocess and object-store call sites in api/src/git, api/src/http, and crates/scope-core/src/object_store. Classify each path by user-visible operation, expected size, retryability, and whether rejection should be 413, 429, or 503.

Output: a small map of admitted IO work. This prevents a generic semaphore from hiding which product flow is actually overloaded.
Start 2

Create the budget boundary

Expose only the shared runtime facade the worker needs: metadata connection setup, object-store setup, job repository APIs, and read-model rebuild handlers. Keep module visibility narrow; do not make every existing API module public just to make the worker compile. Add a service-owned runtime budget facade with named permits for receive-pack, upload-pack, projection builds, and object-store operations. Keep it outside domain behavior; domain code should not know a request was rejected because capacity was exhausted.

Output: one place to configure capacities, timeouts, and backpressure errors for API-owned IO.
Start 3

Bound Git subprocesses

Create durable outbox/job rows with idempotency keys, job kind, target repo/version, payload, state, attempt count, next-run time, lease owner, lease expiry, timestamps, and last error. Route Git command execution through helpers that acquire the right permit, enforce timeout, cap accepted bodies and captured output, truncate diagnostics safely, and clean up staging paths deterministically.

Output: Git failures become bounded, observable API failures instead of tying up request tasks indefinitely.
Start 4

Bound object-store work

When repository persistence saves canonical state, enqueue one idempotent read-model rebuild job for the saved repo version. The request path should no longer execute the rebuild directly once the worker path is proven. Wrap object-store reads, writes, and deletes with operation permits, maximum object sizes, and clear errors. Preserve the current Vec<u8> interface for the first cut; do not introduce streaming until limits prove where it is needed.

Output: S3/blocking-thread pressure and large blob memory pressure have explicit gates.
Start 5

Apply to hot routes first

Teach scope-worker to claim ready jobs with a lease, execute the read-model handler idempotently, mark success, and record retryable failure with backoff and diagnostics. Apply budgets first to Git smart HTTP receive/upload paths and projection Git repo creation, then to source blob reads/writes used by review, history, import, and cleanup paths.

Output: the highest-risk paths get predictable backpressure before lower-volume route polish.
Start 6

Prove saturation behavior

Add local worker startup to the dev process and add scope-worker Railpack/GitHub Actions deployment parallel to the existing API, web, and CLI service deploys. Add tests that force permit exhaustion, subprocess timeout, oversized request bodies, oversized object reads, and subprocess failure. Extend the benchmark report only enough to show status mix and tail behavior under burst load.

Output: green means predictable behavior under pressure, not a guaranteed median speedup.
Guardrail

Do not overbuild Phase 6

Run schema inventory tests, transactional enqueue tests, worker claim/retry tests, route fallback tests, clippy, local-dev API tests, and the Phase 0 benchmark harness. Do not rewrite every Git path, move import/publish to workers, build a full artifact registry, or replace the object-store trait with streaming in the first Phase 6 PR. Land the budget/backpressure shape first.

Output: a small final phase that gives the system a clear overload posture.

This is a discussion artifact, not an approved implementation spec. It reflects the repo state, Linear backlog, merged Phase 0 benchmark work, landed Phase 1 auth-read work, merged Phase 2 cleanup-row work, merged Phase 3 repository-persistence work, merged Phase 4 read-model work, merged Phase 5 worker/outbox work, and Phase 6 bounded-IO direction as of 2026-07-02.

- Treat the terms "task", "thread", "chat", and "conversation" as synonyms when they clearly refer to Codex. Tool names use the term "thread" and Codex uses "task" in the UI. When providing user-facing responses, use "task".
- When the user asks to create, fork, inspect, continue, hand off, pin, archive, rename, or otherwise manage Codex threads, search for the relevant thread tool first: `create_thread`, `fork_thread`, `list_threads`, `read_thread`, `send_message_to_thread`, `handoff_thread`, `set_thread_pinned`, `set_thread_archived`, or `set_thread_title`.

- After a successful `create_thread` call, emit `::created-thread{threadId="..."}` for a created thread or `::created-thread{clientThreadId="..."}` for queued worktree setup on its own line in your final response.

### Inline Code Comments
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive when you need to attach feedback directly to specific code lines.
- Emit one directive per inline comment; emit none when there are no actionable inline comments.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3).
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.

<apps_instructions>
## Apps (Connectors)
Apps (Connectors) can be explicitly triggered in user messages in the format `[$app-name](app://{connector_id})`. Apps can also be implicitly triggered as long as the context suggests usage of available apps.
An app is equivalent to a set of MCP tools within the `codex_apps` MCP.
An installed app's MCP tools are either provided to you already, or can be lazy-loaded through the `tool_search` tool. If `tool_search` is available, the apps that are searchable by `tools_search` will be listed by it.
Do not additionally call list_mcp_resources or list_mcp_resource_templates for apps.
</apps_instructions>

<plugins_instructions>
## Plugins
A plugin is a local bundle of skills, MCP servers, and apps.
### How to use plugins
- Skill naming: If a plugin contributes skills, those skill entries are prefixed with `plugin_name:` in the Skills list.
- MCP naming: Plugin-provided MCP tools keep standard MCP identifiers such as `mcp__server__tool`; use tool provenance to tell which plugin they come from.
- Trigger rules: If the user explicitly names a plugin, prefer capabilities associated with that plugin for that turn.
- Relationship to capabilities: Plugins are not invoked directly. Use their underlying skills, MCP tools, and app tools to help solve the task.
- Relevance: Determine what a plugin can help with from explicit user mention or from the plugin-associated skills, MCP tools, and apps exposed elsewhere in this turn.
- Missing/blocked: If the user requests a plugin that does not have relevant callable capabilities for the task, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
</plugins_instructions>
<multi_agent_mode>Do not spawn sub-agents unless the user or applicable AGENTS.md/skill instructions explicitly ask for sub-agents, delegation, or parallel agent work.</multi_agent_mode>

# AGENTS.md instructions for /home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs

<INSTRUCTIONS>
# Global Agent Preferences

These are durable preferences distilled from local agent history. Project instructions and the current user request always take precedence.

## Working Style

- Prefer action once direction is clear. When the user says to go ahead, implement and verify instead of stopping at another proposal.
- For complex, destructive, architectural, or ambiguous work, first explain the root cause or tradeoff, give a concrete plan, and ask targeted questions if the answer changes the approach.
- Do not assume. Inspect the repo, running app, logs, screenshots, or device state directly when the user asks what is happening.
- Keep the user updated with concrete findings, not generic status. Use simple language when explaining failures or architecture.

## Engineering Taste

- Keep implementations as simple as the scope allows. Less is better than more unless there is a strong reason to add machinery.
- Favor long-term maintainable fixes over local patches. Remove dead code, collapse duplicated logic, and simplify tangled flows while fixing bugs.
- When a system feels strung together, consider subtracting or redesigning the flow instead of layering another workaround on top.
- Tests should be useful regression protection, not coverage theater. Prefer the test level that catches real failures quickly, including E2E for important UI, IPC, connection, or workflow behavior.

## Frontend And Product Work

- Check UI work in the running app yourself. Use browser/Playwright/screenshots or available visual tools to verify layout, alignment, interaction, and loading states.
- Strongly prefer polished, minimal, fast interfaces. Avoid boxy/card-heavy layouts when a clean text, list, or section treatment will work.
- Alignment and spacing matter. Fix visible UI details before calling work done, especially responsive wrapping, centered/left-aligned elements, hover states, and clipped content.
- For redesigns, be willing to be bold and creative, but keep the product usable and wired to existing behavior unless the user explicitly allows backend changes.
- Use named frontend/design/performance skills when the user calls them out, and apply them before implementing.

## Git And Delivery

- Commit, push, merge, or create branches only when the user asks. When asked to commit, verify first and report anything left uncommitted.
- Use a new branch for risky, destructive, or experimental work when requested. Keep focused fixes on the smallest reasonable change surface.
- If CI matters for the request, keep going until it is green or explain the blocker with the exact failing check.
- For firmware, hardware, or device workflows, upload/run/monitor directly when possible and report what happened, including verification gaps.

## Output Expectations

- Summaries should lead with what changed, what was verified, and what remains risky.
- For bug investigations, include the root cause and why the previous behavior failed before describing the fix.
- For plans, make them concrete enough to execute: files or areas to inspect, implementation steps, validation steps, and rollback or migration notes when relevant.

--- project-doc ---

This product is still in pre-alpha and we have zero users and zero valuable data. Every change should be destructive, no legacy or backwards compatible bullshit, this application has no users or releases, treat it as such. If you add backwards compatibility, I will go apeshit on your dumbass.

Maintainer = owner or members

When coding, feel free to spin up medium-level subagents to get non-overlapping code work done and then review outputs at the end. If the work is larger, go ahead and split into worktrees accordingly and coordinate their efforts. Before doing this though please inform the user how you're going to use these subagents.

We want the architecture centered around durable domain code: the layer that defines the core concepts, rules, allowed transitions, invariants, and required side effects independent of any delivery mechanism. Outer layers should stay thin and predictable: translate inputs and outputs, call domain behavior, persist or render results, and surface errors without inventing their own rules.

Keep sources of truth singular, make side effects explicit, and prefer small behavior-owned modules over broad catch-all files. When ownership gets blurry, refactor toward clearer boundaries; when code exists only for speculation, compatibility, or half-owned future surfaces, delete it.

Testing code should be seperate from source code, only combine if you have a good justification to do so.

Please don't use cards for ui, only use them if absolutely necessary.

Once you approach around 1000 LOC in a single file, do an audit of the file and modularize accordingly unless there is a good justification. 

In general, I trust you with refactors as they don't effect the behavior of the application. However, on behavior making changes I want to be very involved and make sure we go slow and methodically. 

Autoreview timeout should be set to 15 minutes

</INSTRUCTIONS>

The web quality gate is fully green: typecheck, API-contract generation, line limits, React Doctor, structural checks, 28 tests, and production build all passed. I’m measuring the final reduction and checking the running-stack path next.

I’m also using three focused subagents now to independently audit the remaining CLI/contracts, test/tooling, and local-stack obligations while I finish the core pass.

payload.message: The current-branch benchmark is green now, including a real first push. That uncovered a stale benchmark assumption: it was using the persistent push token without the required reviewed push-intent header. The harness now exercises the actual first-push flow and completes the intent. I’m entering the mandated 15-minute autoreview closeout after the final LOC reduction lands.

The hard reduction gate is within reach: the latest test-fixture consolidation is preserving every scenario/assertion while removing another block of setup duplication. The current branch remains behavior-green on the browser smoke and benchmark; I’m waiting for the last parallel cleanups to finish before freezing the diff for full tests and autoreview.

The branch is now only 3 lines above the hard gate before the final fixture cleanup completes, with stronger coverage than the original plan assumed: generated SSE types, reusable Rust CI, a 98-line CLI entrypoint, and an isolated Tailscale/Postgres smoke stack. I’m holding the final full test/review freeze until those last concurrent edits finish.

The diff is frozen at 59,456 hand-owned lines: 7,138 lines removed net, clearing the 59,594 hard gate. Formatting and structural searches are clean. I’m starting the final Codex autoreview now in parallel with the complete Postgres-backed Rust matrix; its timeout is capped at the project-required 15 minutes.

Autoreview and the full Rust suite are still running normally; no findings or test failures have surfaced yet. The current branch’s isolated API, worker, and web smoke processes remain healthy while the review examines the frozen diff.

The long gate is still progressing without errors. This is expected: the API suite alone contains 197 unit/workflow cases plus integration targets, and the reviewer is evaluating a large deletion-heavy diff rather than a narrow patch.

The full Postgres-backed Rust matrix is green: 197 API tests, all integration targets, 63 core tests, the boundary test, and worker targets passed. Autoreview itself hit the Codex account usage limit before producing findings; per the review policy, I’m retrying the same engine without changing models.

Three identical Codex review attempts were rejected immediately by the account’s usage cap, so there is no advisory review output to accept or reject. I’m completing the remaining deterministic closeout gates now—strict Clippy, web production build, workflow parsing, final LOC/diff audit—and will report the review limitation explicitly rather than pretending it was clean.

content.text:
# AGENTS.md instructions for /home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs

<INSTRUCTIONS>
These AGENTS.md instructions replace all previously provided AGENTS.md instructions.

# Global Agent Preferences

These are durable preferences distilled from local agent history. Project instructions and the current user request always take precedence.

## Working Style

- Prefer action once direction is clear. When the user says to go ahead, implement and verify instead of stopping at another proposal.
- For complex, destructive, architectural, or ambiguous work, first explain the root cause or tradeoff, give a concrete plan, and ask targeted questions if the answer changes the approach.
- Do not assume. Inspect the repo, running app, logs, screenshots, or device state directly when the user asks what is happening.
- Keep the user updated with concrete findings, not generic status. Use simple language when explaining failures or architecture.

## Engineering Taste

- Keep implementations as simple as the scope allows. Less is better than more unless there is a strong reason to add machinery.
- Favor long-term maintainable fixes over local patches. Remove dead code, collapse duplicated logic, and simplify tangled flows while fixing bugs.
- When a system feels strung together, consider subtracting or redesigning the flow instead of layering another workaround on top.
- Tests should be useful regression protection, not coverage theater. Prefer the test level that catches real failures quickly, including E2E for important UI, IPC, connection, or workflow behavior.

## Frontend And Product Work

- Check UI work in the running app yourself. Use browser/Playwright/screenshots or available visual tools to verify layout, alignment, interaction, and loading states.
- Strongly prefer polished, minimal, fast interfaces. Avoid boxy/card-heavy layouts when a clean text, list, or section treatment will work.
- Alignment and spacing matter. Fix visible UI details before calling work done, especially responsive wrapping, centered/left-aligned elements, hover states, and clipped content.
- For redesigns, be willing to be bold and creative, but keep the product usable and wired to existing behavior unless the user explicitly allows backend changes.
- Use named frontend/design/performance skills when the user calls them out, and apply them before implementing.

## Git And Delivery

- Commit, push, merge, or create branches only when the user asks. When asked to commit, verify first and report anything left uncommitted.
- Use a new branch for risky, destructive, or experimental work when requested. Keep focused fixes on the smallest reasonable change surface.
- If CI matters for the request, keep going until it is green or explain the blocker with the exact failing check.
- For firmware, hardware, or device workflows, upload/run/monitor directly when possible and report what happened, including verification gaps.

## Output Expectations

- Summaries should lead with what changed, what was verified, and what remains risky.
- For bug investigations, include the root cause and why the previous behavior failed before describing the fix.
- For plans, make them concrete enough to execute: files or areas to inspect, implementation steps, validation steps, and rollback or migration notes when relevant.

--- project-doc ---

This product is still in pre-alpha and we have zero users and zero valuable data. Every change should be destructive, no legacy or backwards compatible bullshit, this application has no users or releases, treat it as such. If you add backwards compatibility, I will go apeshit on your dumbass.

Maintainer = owner or members

When coding, feel free to spin up medium-level subagents to get non-overlapping code work done and then review outputs at the end. If the work is larger, go ahead and split into worktrees accordingly and coordinate their efforts. Before doing this though please inform the user how you're going to use these subagents.

We want the architecture centered around durable domain code: the layer that defines the core concepts, rules, allowed transitions, invariants, and required side effects independent of any delivery mechanism. Outer layers should stay thin and predictable: translate inputs and outputs, call domain behavior, persist or render results, and surface errors without inventing their own rules.

Keep sources of truth singular, make side effects explicit, and prefer small behavior-owned modules over broad catch-all files. When ownership gets blurry, refactor toward clearer boundaries; when code exists only for speculation, compatibility, or half-owned future surfaces, delete it.


Please don't use cards for ui, only use them if absolutely necessary.

Once you approach around 1000 LOC in a single file, do an audit of the file and modularize accordingly unless there is a good justification. 

In general, I trust you with refactors as they don't effect the behavior of the application. However, on behavior making changes I want to be very involved and make sure we go slow and methodically. 

Autoreview timeout should be set to 15 minutes

</INSTRUCTIONS>
